reviewed.by: apologia-citations (registered) = 0 errors / nothing fabricated (Scrivener 'The Air We Breathe' 2022, Holland 'Dominion' 2019, Greco-Roman infant exposure, Christians nursing plagues, first-hospitals-for-the-poor [defensible], Christian-led abolition, crucifixion-as-a-slaves-death, imago Dei Gen 1:27 all verified; only quote-marked phrase is Scrivener's own title). apologia-argument + apologia-orthodoxy run via general-purpose using each agent's .md instructions (the registered specialized subagents cannot hot-load this session): orthodoxy CLEAN; argument no-BREAK. Fixes applied from the gates: Holland thesis retargeted 'a gift from Jesus' -> 'a Christian inheritance' (he credits the Christian revolution, not Jesus narrowly); 'first hospitals' -> 'first hospitals for the poor'; kicker 'EVEN SKEPTICS AGREE' -> 'EVEN A SECULAR HISTORIAN'; added a life gesture at the close ('the carpenter they killed still shapes your world') so the legacy arc doesn't leave Jesus merely dead at the cross. Human/pastoral sign-off remains the standing caveat.

voiceover: You believe every human being is equal. That the weak deserve protection. That compassion is good. You think that's obvious. It isn't. For most of history, almost no one believed it — might made right, and the strong ruled. In Rome and Greece, strength was worshipped, slavery was normal, and unwanted babies were left outside to die. Then came a poor Galilean who touched lepers, lifted up women and children, blessed the poor — and died a slave's death on a cross. He taught that every person bears the image of God, that the last would be first, that greatness is serving the weakest. His followers rescued abandoned infants, nursed strangers through plagues, built the first hospitals for the poor, and fought to end slavery. Christians have often betrayed these values — but the very standard we judge them by is his. Glen Scrivener calls it the air we breathe: equality, compassion, dignity — values so common we forget they had to be born. Even the secular historian Tom Holland concludes that human rights and equality aren't facts of nature; they are a Christian inheritance. You don't have to believe in Jesus to live by his values. But the world you love — kinder, freer, more equal than any before it — the carpenter they killed still shapes it.
source: Glen Scrivener, The Air We Breathe (2022); Tom Holland, Dominion (2019)
